text: an eighty-five-year-old man presented with lower abdominal pain as an emergency. There was no past medical history of note. On examination, he had mild deep tenderness on the left iliac fossa. An abdominal X-ray showed dilated colon on the left iliac fossa and a CT scan confirmed the diagnosis of chicle volvulus.

The surgeon inserts a 10mm umbilical port and a 10mm left iliac fossa port for laparoscopy. The laparoscopic view reveals the right and transverse colon displaced to the left side due to the volvulus, with the small bowel on the right. Two additional ports are placed: a 5mm port in the right iliac fossa and another in the epigastrium. Automatic graspers are used to gently untwist the colon in an anti-clockwise direction, repositioning the cecum to the right of the falciform ligament. The left transverse colon is elevated to create space for the small bowel, which is gradually repositioned to the left side by carefully pulling it up from the right.
This process, although appears time consuming in this video, is relieving when all small bowel has been reduced.

For the treatment of cecal vulvas itself in the presence of viable cecum, there may be some debate about whether a right hemicolectomy or a cecopexy should be performed. In this case because of good fitness of the patient a more definitive procedure of right hemicolectomy was performed which has advantage of low recurrence.

The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy by making the ileocolic pedicle taut and conducting parmedial dissection in front of the duodenum. The ileocolic artery is clipped with a hammer lock and divided using an end seal. Blunt dissection is used for further medial mobilization of the colon. A tonsil swab is placed in front of the duodenum as a marker, aiding in the retrieval of the transverse colon and hepatic flexure from the superior side.

The surgeon mobilizes the appendix, cecum, ascending colon, and hepatic flexure from the lateral side, fully freeing the right colon. Despite excess redundancy in the bowel, the dissection proceeds smoothly. The colon is then extracted through the umbilical incision, and an extracorporeal anastomosis is performed.

Relaparoscopy was performed to clear any fluid present inside the abdomen. Patient went home and days have been post up without any complication and he remains well three years from the date of the operation.
